g.data-narration: Welcome to File Handling in C++. Let's explore how to read and write persistent data.
g.text: Reading and Writing Text Files in C++

g.data-narration: File handling enables programs to store data persistently on disk, surviving program termination.
g.text: File handling in C++ allows programs to read from and write to files on disk,
enabling persistent data storage. Data survives program termination.
Essential for: User data, Config settings, Logs, Databases.

g.data-narration: Without file handling, all data is lost when the program closes. Files make applications useful.
C++ provides three main classes: ifstream for reading, ofstream for writing, and fstream for both.
ifstream inherits from istream and is used specifically for reading data from files.
ofstream inherits from ostream and handles writing data to files. It creates new files automatically.
Files must be opened before operations. Always check if the file opened successfully using is_open().
g.text: Close files: stream.close() or let destructor handle it

g.data-narration: File modes determine how files are opened: read, write, append, binary, and more.
Use the extraction operator >> to read whitespace-separated values. It works like cin.
g.text: The >> operator reads whitespace-separated values (like cin):
Note: >> skips whitespace and stops at delimiters. Use while(stream >> var) for EOF handling.

g.data-narration: getline() reads entire lines including spaces. Perfect for reading text with multiple words.
g.text: Reads entire lines including spaces. Discards newline character:
Key Difference: >> stops at whitespace | getline() reads entire line

g.data-narration: Use the insertion operator << to write formatted data to files, just like cout.
g.text: The << operator writes formatted data to files (like cout):
Always close files to flush buffer: outFile.close()

g.data-narration: Always verify file operations using is_open(), good(), eof(), and fail() member functions.
Remember: include fstream, use ifstream for reading, ofstream for writing, check is_open().
g.text: • Include <fstream> header for all file operations
• ifstream = reading, ofstream = writing, fstream = both
• Always check is_open() before file operations

g.data-narration: Avoid common mistakes: forgetting fstream header, not checking file open, confusing >> with getline().
g.text: • ios::out implies ios::trunc by default - use ios::app to preserve data
• Common mistake: Using >> leaves newline in buffer; getline() discards it
